title: Evidence for altermagnetic order in Cr-doped FeSb2
authors: A K M Ashiquzzaman Shawon, Eoghan Downey, Shane Smolenski, Thomas J. Hicken, Amir Henderson, Mingyu Xu, Trisha Musall, Rafael Lopes Sabainsk, Yuan Zhu, Weiwei Xie, Elena Gati, Lu Li, Zurab Guguchia, Na Hyun Jo
abstract: Altermagnets are a class of materials with compensated magnetic moments, in which spin sublattices are related by specific symmetries other than inversion or translation. This allows time-reversal symmetry to be broken without a net magnetization. Here, we synthesize single crystals of Fe1-xCrxSb2 and investigate their electrical transport and magnetic properties, with a focus on Fe0.85Cr0.15Sb2. Magnetization measurements suggest spin-compensated ordering below ~ 3.5 K, where magnetic moments align along the crystallographic b-direction. Transport measurements reveal a crossover from large positive to negative magnetoresistance, while an anomalous Hall response emerges below 3.5 K, indicating time-reversal symmetry breaking below TN. Muon spin relaxation measurements demonstrate bulk magnetic order below 3.5 K, confirming that the low temperature ordering is intrinsic rather than due to an impurity phase. These results support a potential altermagnetic ground state in Cr-doped FeSb2 with time-reversal symmetry breaking without net magnetization.
summary: This paper provides experimental evidence for altermagnetism in Cr-doped FeSb2 by demonstrating a spin-compensated magnetic state that breaks time-reversal symmetry. The observation of a spontaneous anomalous Hall effect and bulk magnetic order supports the theoretical prediction of a d-wave altermagnetic ground state.
main_problem: To investigate the magnetic ground state of Cr-doped FeSb2 and provide experimental evidence for the existence of altermagnetic order.
main_result: The study identifies a spin-compensated magnetic ground state with broken time-reversal symmetry, evidenced by a spontaneous anomalous Hall effect and bulk magnetic order below 3.5 K.
method: Single crystals of Fe1-xCrxSb2 were grown via the Sb-flux method and characterized using X-ray diffraction, magnetometry, electrical transport measurements, and Muon Spin Relaxation (uSR).
model_or_system: The physical system is Cr-doped FeSb2 (specifically Fe0.85Cr0.15Sb2), which possesses an orthorhombic marcasite-type crystal structure and is a candidate for d-wave altermagnetism.
key_observables: Magnetic susceptibility, magnetization, longitudinal resistivity, Hall resistivity, magnetoresistance, and muon spin relaxation decay rates.
important_parameters: Néel temperature (TN) of approximately 3.5 K, Cr-doping concentration (x), and magnetic anisotropy along the b-axis.
main_assumptions: The magnetic order is assumed to be intrinsic and bulk-like rather than due to impurity phases, a claim supported by uSR.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, XRD patterns, and lattice parameters; Fig 2 displays temperature-dependent resistivity, magnetoresistance, Hall resistivity, and magnetization; Fig 3 presents uSR spectra under zero and longitudinal fields.
paper_structure: The paper begins with crystal growth and structural characterization, followed by magnetic and transport property measurements, then presents muon spin relaxation data to confirm bulk order, and concludes with a discussion of the altermagnetic state.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (A) Crystal structure of orthorhombic Fe1−xCrxSb2, viewed along the crystallographic c-direction, is overlaid with the proposed altermagnetic magnetic structure where time- reversal symmetry is coupled to the rotational symmetry. (B) Powder X-ray diffraction patterns for each composition confirm the marcasite structure, with minority contributions from surface Sb-flux (⋆) and Si sample holder (□). With in- creasing Cr-content, peaks shift to lower 2θ angles, confirming successful doping. (C) Lattice parameters, collected using Ri- etveld refinement, progressively increase with increasing Cr- content, further confirming homogenous doping. Dashed lines serve as a guide to the eye.

FIG. 2. Electrical and magnetic properties in the Fe0.85Cr0.15Sb2 samples: (A) Temperature-dependent resistivity measured along [001] is shown under zero (solid lines) and 14 T (dashed lines) perpendicular magnetic field applied along [110]. (B) Temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility is shown for fields applied along [010] and [001], measured with µ0H = 0.1 T. A Curie plot is shown in the inset. (C) MR measured along [010] (µ0H along [101]) and (D) MR measured along [001] (µ0H along [110]) are shown, highlighting anisotropy. (E) Hall resistivity with I along [010] (µ0H along [101]) is shown. (F) Hall resistivity with current along [001] (magnetic field along [110]) exhibits anomalous...

FIG. 3. (A) Weak transverse field (TF) muon spectra reveal asymmetry at 7.5 K and below. (B) Magnetic volume fraction, extracted by fitting weak transverse field spectra, shows nearly the entire sample is magnetically ordered below TN. (C) Zero field (ZF) µSR spectra also reveal muon polarization loss without oscillations. (D) ZF spectrum at 1.53 K shows two distinct decay rates, one fast and one slow, whose temperature evolution is shown in (E). (F) An applied longitudinal magnetic field up to 0.2 T decouples the fast component, causing muon polarization to increase with field.
